sections.heading: Step 1: Create a  configuration file and specify the  type.
Step 2: Connect to the Atlas CLI
sections.body:
In your terminal, connect to your cluster from the
Atlas CLI. To learn more, see
[Connect from the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/connect-atlas-cli).

sections.heading: Step 3: Create a  index.
sections.body:
Run the [atlas clusters search indexes create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-clusters-search-indexes-create)
command in your terminal, replacing `<clusterName>` with the
name of the cluster, and `<path-to-file>` with the
path to the `search-index.json` file:

```shell
atlas clusters search indexes create --clusterName <cluster-name> --file <path-to-file>

```

The MongoDB Search `geo` type takes the following parameters:

| Option      | Type    | Necessity | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                               | Default |
| ----------- | ------- | --------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------- |
| type        | string  | Required  | Human-readable label that identifies this field type. UI value must be Geo and JSON (Javascript Object Notation) value must be geo.                                                                                                       |         |
| indexShapes | boolean | Optional  | Flag that indicates whether to index shapes. By default, MongoDB Search:Indexes points, even when nested.Doesn't index shape geometries such as lines and polygons.Value can be:true to index shapes and pointsfalse to index only points | false   |

The following index definition examples use collections in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets). After you
[Load Sample Data Into Atlas](load-sample-data), you can use
these examples to index fields as the dateFacet type.

To learn how to set up and run the following example code, see
Define the Index for the token Type.

The following index definition for the `sample_airbnb.listingsAndReviews` collection
indexes the `address.location` field as the `geo` type. This index allows
you to query against that field by using the MongoDB Search [geoShape](geoshape-ref)
and [geoWithin](geowithin-ref) operators.

```json
{
  "name": "default",
  "collectionName": "listingsAndReviews",
  "database": "sample_airbnb",
  "mappings": {
    "dynamic": false,
    "fields": {
      "address": {
        "type": "document",
        "fields": {
          "location": {
            "type": "geo",
            "indexShapes": true 
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

To learn more about the operators you can use to query the `geo` type, see the following operator reference pages:

- [geoShape](geoshape-ref)

- [geoWithin](geowithin-ref)

You can use the MongoDB Search `geo` type to index geographic points and shape
coordinates. For this type, the indexed field must be a [GeoJSON](manual/reference/geojson) object. You can use the [geoShape](geoshape-ref) and
[geoWithin](geowithin-ref) operators to query indexed fields of type `geo`.

dateFacet type is not included in the dynamic mapping by default. To index fields
as dateFacet type, you can use [dynamic mappings](static-dynamic-mappings) or configure a `typeSet` to include dateFacet in dynamic mapping.

sections.heading: Step 1: Connect to your cluster using .
sections.body:
Open [Compass](https://www.mongodb.com/try/download/compass) and connect to your cluster. For
detailed instructions, see [Compass](atlas-connect-via-compass).

sections.heading: Step 2: Specify the database and collection.
sections.body: On the Database screen, click the name of the database, then click the name of the collection.

sections.heading: Step 3: Create the  index.
Step 1: Connect to the deployment by using .
sections.body:
In your terminal, connect to your Atlas cloud-hosted
deployment or local deployment from [mongosh](mongodb-shell). For detailed
instructions on how to connect, see
[Connect to a Deployment](mongodb-shell/connect).

sections.heading: Step 2: Switch to the database that contains the collection for which you want to create the index.
sections.body:
**Input:**

```shell
 use <database>
```

**Output:**

```shell
switched to db <database>
```

sections.heading: Step 3: Run the  method to create the index.
Step 1: Set up the C project.
sections.body:
In your terminal, navigate to where you want to create your application,
then run the following command to create a directory called
`atlas-search-project` for this project:

```shell
# Create a new directory for the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
```

Add the C driver to your project by following the instructions in the
[MongoDB C Driver documentation](languages/c/c-driver/current/get-started).

codeBlocks.content:
# Create a new directory for the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
Step 3: Set up a CMake application
sections.body:
To configure your application, create a `CMakeLists.txt` file in
your project directory. Then, add the following code to the file:

```txt
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.11)
project(atlas-search-index LANGUAGES C)
add_executable (index.out create_index.c)
find_package(mongoc <version> REQUIRED)
target_link_libraries(index.out mongoc::mongoc)

```

The preceding code performs the following actions:

- Configures a C project.

- Creates a `index.out` executable for your application.

- Finds and requires the C driver. Replace the `<version>`
  placeholder with your C driver version, such as `2.0.0`.

- Links the program to the `libmongoc` library.

> **Note:** In the sample `CMakeLists.txt` file, the `mongoc::mongoc` target
> points to either the static library or the shared library.
> The library type depends on which one is available and
> whichever type the user specifies in the `MONGOC_DEFAULT_IMPORTED_LIBRARY_TYPE`
> CMake configuration setting. If you don't set this value and
> both library types are available, `mongoc::mongoc` uses
> the static library.
>
> You can use the `mongoc::static` target to explicitly use the
> static library or the `mongoc::shared` target to use the shared
> library.

codeBlocks.content:
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.11)
project(atlas-search-index LANGUAGES C)
add_executable (index.out create_index.c)
find_package(mongoc <version> REQUIRED)
target_link_libraries(index.out mongoc::mongoc)

sections.heading: Step 4: Create the index.
Step 1: Set up and initialize the .NET/C# project.
sections.body:
In your terminal, navigate to where you want to create your application,
then run the following command to create a directory called
`atlas-search-project` and initialize your project in that directory:

```shell
# Create a new directory and initialize the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
dotnet new console

# Add the MongoDB .NET/C# Driver to your project
dotnet add package MongoDB.Driver
```

For more detailed installation instructions, see the
[MongoDB C# Driver documentation](csharp/get-started).

codeBlocks.content:
# Create a new directory and initialize the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
dotnet new console

# Add the MongoDB .NET/C# Driver to your project
dotnet add package MongoDB.Driver

In your terminal, navigate to where you want to create your application,
then run the following command to create a directory called
`atlas-search-project` and initialize your project in that directory:

```shell

# Add the MongoDB Go Driver to your project

For more detailed installation instructions, see the

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
Step 3: Create the index.
Step 1: Set up your Java project with the MongoDB Java driver.
sections.body:
In your IDE, create a new [Maven](https://maven.apache.org/) or
[Gradle](https://gradle.org/) project. Add the Bill of
Materials (BOM) for MongoDB JVM artifacts to your project to
organize dependency versions. The BOM simplifies dependency
management by ensuring that you maintain consistent and compatible
versions of dependencies, such as between the Java driver and
the core driver library. Use the BOM to avoid version conflicts
and simplify upgrades.Select from the following **Maven** and **Gradle** tabs
to view instructions for adding the BOM for each dependency manager:MavenAdd the following code to the `dependencyManagement` list in your
`pom.xml` file:```xml
<dependencyManagement>
    <dependencies>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.mongodb</groupId>
            <artifactId>mongodb-driver-bom</artifactId>
            <version>5.5.1</version>
            <type>pom</type>
            <scope>import</scope>
        </dependency>
    </dependencies>
</dependencyManagement>
```GradleAdd the following code to dependencies list in your
`build.gradle` file:```groovy
dependencies {
    implementation(platform("org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-bom:5.5.1"))
}
```To view a list of dependencies that the BOM manages, see
the [mongodb-driver-bom dependency listing](https://mvnrepository.com/artifact/org.mongodb/mongodb-driver-bom/5.51)
on the Maven Repository website.After adding the BOM, select from the following **Maven** and **Gradle** tabs to view
instructions for adding the MongoDB Java Sync Driver as a dependency
in your project:MavenAdd the following code to the `dependencies` array in your Maven
project's `pom.xml` file:```xml
<dependencies>
    <dependency>    
      <groupId>org.mongodb</groupId>    
      <artifactId>mongodb-driver-sync</artifactId>    
      <version>4.11.1</version>    
    </dependency>
</dependencies>

```GradleAdd the following to the `dependencies` array in your Gradle
project's `build.gradle` file:```json
dependencies {
   // MongoDB Java Sync Driver v4.11.0 or later
   implementation 'org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-sync'
}

```Run your package manager to install the dependencies to your project.For more detailed installation instructions and version compatibility, see
the [MongoDB Java Driver documentation](java/sync/current/get-started).

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.

Step 1: Initialize your Node.js project.
sections.body:

# Create a new directory and initialize the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
npm init -y

# Add the MongoDB Node.js Driver to your project
npm install mongodb
```

For detailed installation instructions, see the
[MongoDB Node Driver documentation](node/get-started).

codeBlocks.content:

mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
Step 3: Create the index.
Step 1: Set up the Python project.
sections.body:
```shell
# Create a new directory for the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project

# Install PyMongo
pip install pymongo
```

For detailed installation instructions, see the
[MongoDB PyMongo Driver documentation](python/pymongo-driver/current/get-started).

codeBlocks.content:
# Create a new directory for the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project

# Install PyMongo
pip install pymongo

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
sections.body:
Create a `create_index.py` file in your project directory,
and copy and paste the following code into the file.```python
from pymongo import MongoClient
from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

# Connect to your Atlas deployment
uri = "<connection-string>"
client = MongoClient(uri)

database = client["<database>"]
collection = database["<collection>"]

# Create the MongoDB Search index definition for the geo field
search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
    definition={
        "mappings": {
            "dynamic": true|false,
            "fields": {
                "<field-name>":{
                   "type": "geo",
                   "indexShapes": true|false
                }
            }
        },
    },
    name="default",
)

# Create the index
result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
print(f"New index name: {result}")

```Replace `<connection-string>` with the connection string for your
Atlas cluster or local Atlas deployment.Atlas ClusterYour connection string should use the following format:```
mongodb+srv://<db_username>:<db_password>@<clusterName>.<hostname>.mongodb.net
```To learn more, see [Driver](connect-via-driver).Local or Self-ManagedYour connection string should use the
following format:```
mongodb://localhost:<port-number>/?directConnection=true
```To learn more, see [Connection Strings](manual/reference/connection-string).

sections.heading: Step 3: Create the index.
sections.body:
**Input:**

```shell
python create_index.py
```

**Output:**

```
New index name: default
```

The MongoDB Search `geo` type takes the following parameters:

| Option      | Type    | Necessity | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                               | Default |
| ----------- | ------- | --------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------- |
| type        | string  | Required  | Human-readable label that identifies this field type. UI value must be Geo and JSON (Javascript Object Notation) value must be geo.                                                                                                       |         |
| indexShapes | boolean | Optional  | Flag that indicates whether to index shapes. By default, MongoDB Search:Indexes points, even when nested.Doesn't index shape geometries such as lines and polygons.Value can be:true to index shapes and pointsfalse to index only points | false   |

The following index definition examples use collections in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets). After you
[Load Sample Data Into Atlas](load-sample-data), you can use
these examples to index fields as the dateFacet type.

To learn how to set up and run the following example code, see
Define the Index for the token Type.

The following index definition for the `sample_airbnb.listingsAndReviews` collection
indexes the `address.location` field as the `geo` type. This index allows
you to query against that field by using the MongoDB Search [geoShape](geoshape-ref)
and [geoWithin](geowithin-ref) operators.

```python
from pymongo import MongoClient
from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

# Connect to your Atlas deployment
uri = "<connection-string>"
client = MongoClient(uri)

database = client["sample_airbnb"]
collection = database["listingsAndReviews"]

# Create the MongoDB Search index definition for the geo field
search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
    definition={
        "mappings": {
            "dynamic": false,
            "fields": {
                "address": {
                    "type": "document",
                    "fields": {
                        "location": {
                            "type": "geo",
                            "indexShapes": true
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        },
    },
    name="default",
)

# Create the index
result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
print(f"New index name: {result}")
```

To learn more about the operators you can use to query the `geo` type, see the following operator reference pages:

- [geoShape](geoshape-ref)

- [geoWithin](geowithin-ref)

You can use the MongoDB Search `geo` type to index geographic points and shape
coordinates. For this type, the indexed field must be a [GeoJSON](manual/reference/geojson) object. You can use the [geoShape](geoshape-ref) and
[geoWithin](geowithin-ref) operators to query indexed fields of type `geo`.

dateFacet type is not included in the dynamic mapping by default. To index fields
as dateFacet type, you can use [dynamic mappings](static-dynamic-mappings) or configure a `typeSet` to include dateFacet in dynamic mapping.

sections.body:
Run the [atlas deployments search indexes create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-deployments-search-indexes-create)
command in your terminal, replacing `<path-to-file>` with the
path to the `search-index.json` file:

```shell
atlas deployments search indexes create --file <path-to-file>

```

The MongoDB Search `geo` type takes the following parameters:

| Option      | Type    | Necessity | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                               | Default |
| ----------- | ------- | --------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------- |
| type        | string  | Required  | Human-readable label that identifies this field type. UI value must be Geo and JSON (Javascript Object Notation) value must be geo.                                                                                                       |         |
| indexShapes | boolean | Optional  | Flag that indicates whether to index shapes. By default, MongoDB Search:Indexes points, even when nested.Doesn't index shape geometries such as lines and polygons.Value can be:true to index shapes and pointsfalse to index only points | false   |

The following index definition examples use collections in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets). After you
[Load Sample Data Into Atlas](load-sample-data), you can use
these examples to index fields as the dateFacet type.

To learn how to set up and run the following example code, see
Define the Index for the token Type.

The following index definition for the `sample_airbnb.listingsAndReviews` collection
indexes the `address.location` field as the `geo` type. This index allows
you to query against that field by using the MongoDB Search [geoShape](geoshape-ref)
and [geoWithin](geowithin-ref) operators.

```json
{
  "name": "default",
  "collectionName": "listingsAndReviews",
  "database": "sample_airbnb",
  "mappings": {
    "dynamic": false,
    "fields": {
      "address": {
        "type": "document",
        "fields": {
          "location": {
            "type": "geo",
            "indexShapes": true 
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

To learn more about the operators you can use to query the `geo` type, see the following operator reference pages:

- [geoShape](geoshape-ref)

- [geoWithin](geowithin-ref)

You can use the MongoDB Search `geo` type to index geographic points and shape
coordinates. For this type, the indexed field must be a [GeoJSON](manual/reference/geojson) object. You can use the [geoShape](geoshape-ref) and
[geoWithin](geowithin-ref) operators to query indexed fields of type `geo`.

dateFacet type is not included in the dynamic mapping by default. To index fields
as dateFacet type, you can use [dynamic mappings](static-dynamic-mappings) or configure a `typeSet` to include dateFacet in dynamic mapping.

npm init -y

# Add the MongoDB Node.js Driver to your project
npm install mongodb
```

For detailed installation instructions, see the
[MongoDB Node Driver documentation](node/get-started).

codeBlocks.content:
# Create a new directory for the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project

# Install PyMongo
pip install pymongo

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
sections.body:
Create a `create_index.py` file in your project directory,
and copy and paste the following code into the file.```python
from pymongo import MongoClient
from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

# Connect to your Atlas deployment
uri = "<connection-string>"
client = MongoClient(uri)

database = client["<database>"]
collection = database["<collection>"]

# Create the MongoDB Search index definition for the geo field
search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
    definition={
        "mappings": {
            "dynamic": true|false,
            "fields": {
                "<field-name>":{
                   "type": "geo",
                   "indexShapes": true|false
                }
            }
        },
    },
    name="default",
)

# Create the index
result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
print(f"New index name: {result}")

```Replace `<connection-string>` with the connection string for your
Atlas cluster or local Atlas deployment.Atlas ClusterYour connection string should use the following format:```
mongodb+srv://<db_username>:<db_password>@<clusterName>.<hostname>.mongodb.net
```To learn more, see [Driver](connect-via-driver).Local or Self-ManagedYour connection string should use the
following format:```
mongodb://localhost:<port-number>/?directConnection=true
```To learn more, see [Connection Strings](manual/reference/connection-string).

sections.heading: Step 3: Create the index.
sections.body:
**Input:**

```shell
python create_index.py
```

**Output:**

```
New index name: default
```

The MongoDB Search `geo` type takes the following parameters:

| Option      | Type    | Necessity | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                               | Default |
| ----------- | ------- | --------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------- |
| type        | string  | Required  | Human-readable label that identifies this field type. UI value must be Geo and JSON (Javascript Object Notation) value must be geo.                                                                                                       |         |
| indexShapes | boolean | Optional  | Flag that indicates whether to index shapes. By default, MongoDB Search:Indexes points, even when nested.Doesn't index shape geometries such as lines and polygons.Value can be:true to index shapes and pointsfalse to index only points | false   |

The following index definition examples use collections in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets). After you
[Load Sample Data Into Atlas](load-sample-data), you can use
these examples to index fields as the dateFacet type.

To learn how to set up and run the following example code, see
Define the Index for the token Type.

The following index definition for the `sample_airbnb.listingsAndReviews` collection
indexes the `address.location` field as the `geo` type. This index allows
you to query against that field by using the MongoDB Search [geoShape](geoshape-ref)
and [geoWithin](geowithin-ref) operators.

```python
from pymongo import MongoClient
from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

# Connect to your Atlas deployment
uri = "<connection-string>"
client = MongoClient(uri)

database = client["sample_airbnb"]
collection = database["listingsAndReviews"]

# Create the MongoDB Search index definition for the geo field
search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
    definition={
        "mappings": {
            "dynamic": false,
            "fields": {
                "address": {
                    "type": "document",
                    "fields": {
                        "location": {
                            "type": "geo",
                            "indexShapes": true
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        },
    },
    name="default",
)

# Create the index
result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
print(f"New index name: {result}")
```

To learn more about the operators you can use to query the `geo` type, see the following operator reference pages:

- [geoShape](geoshape-ref)

- [geoWithin](geowithin-ref)
